html:
Soluciones Cuatroochenta: A Tiny Spanish Software Roll-Up With Better Numbers Than Its Reputation

Cuatroochenta is no longer the messy, hope-based SaaS roll-up implied by the old thesis. The 2025 filing shows a real inflection: revenue grew 32.5% to €36.5 million, EBITDA grew 27.9% to €4.1 million, net income reached €2.0 million, and ARR rose 18.9% to €23.0 million. Organic growth was 23%, with another 11% from the 2025 acquisitions of 3ipunt and MP Services. Recurring revenue is now 63% of sales. This is not a melting ice cube.

The market still treats 480S as an illiquid BME Growth acquisition vehicle with leverage and low visibility. That concern is not imaginary, but it misses the change in quality. The business is now more cybersecurity and recurring revenue than generic consulting. Cybersecurity is 45% of revenue, €15.8 million of ARR, and grew ARR 40% in 2025. VAR is 29% of revenue and grew ARR 36%. ISV is slower, but includes sticky vertical software such as FAMA and CheckingPlan. Professional services are only 10% of revenue, not the core valuation driver.

The most important nuance is the balance sheet. Reported net debt rose to €9.5 million, or 2.3x EBITDA, after two acquisitions. But €5.4 million of debt is convertible paper from Inveready and Eiffel, and management says leverage excluding convertibles is only 1.0x EBITDA. Eiffel’s €2.0 million convertible was issued at an €18 conversion price, with Eiffel also buying stock in the market. That matters because the scary version of the thesis was “debt-funded roll-up runs out of road.” The current version is “profitable small-cap software platform has enough leverage to create equity torque, but not enough to make refinancing the whole thesis.”

The hidden asset is the forward revenue base. Customer prepayments and deferred revenue were €9.7 million at year-end, and the company expects 99% of that deferred-license balance to be recognized in 2026. Operating cash flow was €5.0 million in 2025, similar to 2024 despite acquisition activity. If M&A slows, maintenance capex is much lower than reported investment cash flow, and the business can self-fund growth while deleveraging.

The official 2030 base case is also more useful than it looks. Management targets €70 million of revenue and €10 million of EBITDA by 2030, explicitly excluding further M&A. That implies 17% organic CAGR from 2024 and a roughly 14% EBITDA margin. A market that believes even half of that will not keep valuing the company like a fragile micro-cap services shop.

At €17.00, the market cap is about €46.5 million. Using 2025 net debt, enterprise value is roughly €56 million. On 2026 analyst estimates, the stock is around 16x earnings, about 10x forward EBITDA, and below 1.3x forward sales. That is not obviously “cheap” for a generic IT services company. It is cheap for a 20% grower with 63% recurring revenue, 44% international sales, cybersecurity mix shift, and visible deferred revenue.

The variant perception is that Cuatroochenta is not a broken roll-up anymore. It is a subscale European software holding company transitioning from acquisition digestion into compounding. The stock’s small size, Spanish listing, low float, insider block trades, and retail skepticism keep it ignored. Retail forums are still focused on illiquidity, founder sales, and the memory of the stock falling from prior highs. That is exactly why the opportunity exists: the business has improved faster than the shareholder base.

The main bear point is governance and capital allocation. Founders sold blocks in December and February, although those sales also brought in Eiffel and Virtus. Management also says it will keep analyzing acquisitions, so the market cannot assume all cash flow will go to debt reduction. If the next deals are expensive or integration-heavy, the FCF thesis weakens quickly.

Still, downside protection is better than in the prior draft. The company has 1,900 clients, meaningful public-sector and enterprise references, €23 million of ARR, positive net income, and operating cash generation. This is not a zero-or-hero biotech-style microcap. The downside is more likely multiple compression and illiquidity than insolvency, unless management materially overreaches on M&A.

Targets

Bear: €10.00, down 41% from €17.00. This assumes organic growth slows, ARR quality disappoints, M&A spend continues, and the stock is valued as a levered BME Growth services roll-up.

Base: €23.00, up 35%. This assumes 2026 revenue near €43 million, EBITDA around €5 million to €5.5 million, modest deleveraging, and a partial rerating to roughly 11x to 12x forward EBITDA.

Bull: €35.00, up 106%. This assumes cybersecurity remains the growth engine, ARR compounds high teens, convertibles become equity rather than stress, and the market starts underwriting the €70 million revenue and €10 million EBITDA 2030 path.

At today’s €17.00 price, 480S is a small, illiquid, underfollowed software compounder where the market still prices the old risk profile while the latest filings show a better forward business.
